file_name: GentechHoldingsInc_20190808_1-A_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_11776814_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_Distributor Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: Company shall package the Products for shipment in accordance with Distributor's instructions. All shipments of Products for Customers in the Territory, and any freight and shipping costs related thereto, will be Distributor's responsibility. All shipments will be prepared and shipped from Company's facility.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall attempt to package the Products for shipment in general accordance with Distributor's instructions. All shipments of Products for Customers in the Territory, and any freight and shipping costs related thereto, are generally the Distributor's responsibility. All shipments may be prepared and shipped from Company's facility.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall package" to "shall attempt to package in general accordance", "will be" to "are generally" and "will be prepared and shipped" to "may be prepared and shipped" introduces significant ambiguity. It's now unclear to what extent the company is obligated to follow the distributor's packaging instructions, who is ultimately responsible for shipping costs, and whether shipments are guaranteed to originate from the Company's facility. This leaves the terms open to interpretation and potential disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC governs the sale of goods, including shipping and payment terms. By making the shipping and packaging obligations vague, the modified text contradicts the UCC's requirements for clear and definite terms in sales contracts. For example, UCC § 2-319 defines specific shipping terms like "F.O.B." (Free on Board), which clearly allocates responsibility for shipping costs and risk of loss. The ambiguous language in the modified contract makes it difficult to determine the parties' obligations, potentially leading to disputes over shipping costs and responsibility for damaged goods, conflicting with the UCC's intent to provide a clear framework for commercial transactions.
perturbation.location: 2. ORDERS, PRICE, TERMS OF SALE & PAYMENT. (c)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-319. F.O.B. and F.A.S. Terms. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-319. F.O.B. and F.A.S. Terms. (1)Unless otherwise agreed the term F.O.B. (which means "free on board") at a named place, even though used only in connection with the stated price, is a delivery term under which (a) when the term is F.O.B. the place of shipment, the seller must at that place ship the goods in the manner provided in this Article (Section 2-504 ) and bear the expense and risk of putting them into the possession of the carrier; or (b) when the term is F.O.B. the place of destination, the seller must at his own expense and risk transport the goods to that place and there tender delivery of them in the manner provided in this Article (Section 2-503 ); (c) when under either (a) or (b) the term is also F.O.B. vessel, car or other vehicle, the seller must in addition at his own expense and risk load the goods on board. If the term is F.O.B. vessel the buyer must name the vessel and in an appropriate case the seller must comply with the provisions of this Article on the form of bill of lading (Section 2-323 ). (2) Unless otherwise agreed the term F.A.S. vessel (which means "free alongside") at a named port, even though used only in connection with the stated price, is a delivery term under which the seller must (a) at his own expense and risk deliver the goods alongside the vessel in the manner usual in that port or on a dock designated and provided by the buyer ; and (b) obtain and tender a receipt for the goods in exchange for which the carrier is under a duty to issue a bill of lading. (3) Unless otherwise agreed in any case falling within subsection (1)(a) or (c) or subsection (2) the buyer must seasonably give any needed instructions for making delivery, including when the term is F.A.S. or F.O.B. the loading berth of the vessel and in an appropriate case its name and sailing date. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities regarding the company's obligations for packaging and shipping, potentially conflicting with the UCC's requirement for clear terms in sales contracts. The original text uses definitive language such as "shall package" and "will be," creating clear obligations. The changed text weakens these obligations by using terms like "shall attempt to package in general accordance," "are generally," and "may be," making it difficult to enforce specific performance. This ambiguity could lead to disputes over shipping costs and responsibility for damaged goods, which is in line with the UCC's goal of establishing a clear framework for commercial transactions. The LLM explanation is supported by Snippet 1's reference to UCC § 2-319, which defines clear shipping terms, which the modified text does not provide.

perturbation.original_text: The Distributor will use best reasonable efforts to distribute the Products in the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: The Distributor will consider using reasonable efforts to distribute the Products in the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "best reasonable efforts" to "consider using reasonable efforts" significantly weakens the distributor's obligation. The original phrase implies a strong commitment to maximizing distribution. The modified phrase suggests a more passive approach, where the distributor merely contemplates using some degree of effort, without a guarantee of actual action or a standard for measuring their performance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Every contract in Florida (and most US jurisdictions) contains an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act honestly and in a way that doesn't frustrate the purpose of the contract. By weakening the distributor's obligation to distribute the products, the modified text potentially violates this implied covenant. If the distributor only "considers" using reasonable efforts, they may not be fulfilling their duty to promote the products effectively, undermining the core purpose of the distributorship agreement, which is to sell the Company's products in the territory. This creates a question of whether the distributor is acting in good faith to achieve the intended outcome of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the distributor's obligation, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original text, "best reasonable efforts," implies a strong commitment. The changed text, "consider using reasonable efforts," suggests a passive approach. This undermines the contract's purpose, which is to distribute the products. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines "good faith" as encompassing "honest dealing" and "faithful performance of duties". The change suggests that the distributor may not be faithfully performing their duties, which constitutes a failure to act in good faith.
